content.full_text:
April 16, 1992


Gregory D. Thatch
1730 I Street, Suite 220
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                        Re: Your Request for Advice
                                            Our File No. I-92-125

Dear Mr. Thatch:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice
concerning the duties of members of private industry councils
(PICs) under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act"). Your request is general in nature and
seeks general guidance regarding application of the provisions of
the Act. Accordingly, we are providing you with informal
assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.²

     Our jurisdiction is limited to application of the provisions
of the Act. Therefore, we make no comments regarding other
provisions of law such as Government Code Section 1090. In
addition, our advice is prospective in nature and should not be
interpreted as an evaluation of any past conduct.

QUESTIONS

     1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
members of a private industry council (PIC) to disqualify
themselves from participating in Job Training Partnership Act
(JTPA) funding decisions when the member or the member's employer
has entered into an on-the-job training contract with a program
operator which is soliciting funds?


________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(C).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 2

2. What must a PIC member do to comply with the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act when voting on JTPA funding
decisions when the member or the member's employer has entered
into an on-the-job training contract with a program operator which
is soliciting funds? Specifically:

a. Must the PIC member abstain from voting only on
awards of JTPA funds to program operators with which the member or
the member's employer has had a contract?

b. Must the PIC member abstain from voting on awards of
JTPA funds to all program operators if the member or the member's
employer has contracted with any of the program operators?

c. Must the PIC member abstain from participating in
all discussions of requests for proposals if the member or the
member's employer has contracted with any of the program operators
responding to the request for proposals at issue?

CONCLUSIONS

1. and 2. Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act, public officials, including PIC members, are disqualified
from participating in governmental decisions which will have a
material financial effect on their economic interests.
Accordingly, members of PICs may not participate in funding
decisions which will have a material financial effect on the
member or the member's employer.

FACTS

A. Job Training Partnership Act

In 1982, the federal government enacted the Job Training
Partnership Act (JTPA) which established programs to prepare youth and
unskilled adults for entry into the labor force and to afford job
training to those economically disadvantaged individuals and other
individuals facing serious barriers to employment, who are in
special need of such training to obtain productive employment."
(29 U.S.C. Section 1501.) To accomplish these goals, the
legislation provided that each state would be divided into
appropriate Service Delivery Areas (SDAs) "to promote effective
delivery of job training services" and to be "consistent with
areas in which related services are provided under other State or
Federal programs." (29 U.S.C Section 1511.) As such, an SDA
could be comprised of "any consortium of contiguous units of local
government with an aggregate population of 200,000 or more which
serves a substantial part of a labor market area." (Id.)

1. Sacramento Employment and Training Agency

Pursuant to a Joint Powers Agreement between the City of
Sacramento and the County of Sacramento, the SDA for the city and
the county is the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 3

(SETA). SETA is operated under the control and supervision of the
SETA governing board (the "board") comprised of two members of the
Sacramento County Board of Supervisors, two members of the City
Council of the City of Sacramento and one public member. In
addition to serving as the local SDA under the JTPA, SETA also
administers funds under other federal and state programs such as
Head Start, the Community Service Block Grant Act, Greater Avenues
for Independence, the Refugee Targeted Assistance Program and the
Stewart B. McKinney Homeless Act.

2. Private Industry Council

The JTPA further provided for the creation, in each SDA, of a
PIC, appointed by the local SDA and comprised of "representatives
of the private sector" and "representatives of educational
agencies, organized labor, rehabilitation agencies, community-
based organizations, economic development agencies, and the public
employment service."1 (29 U.S.C. Section 1512.) Consistent with
the requirements of the JTPA, the majority of the PIC members are
from the private sector and the chairperson of the board is
selected from among the private sector members. In recognition of
the quasi-governmental status of PICs, it has previously been
determined by the office of the Attorney General that members of
the PIC are public officials who must comply with the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Government Code. (65 Ops. Att. Gen. 41
(1982).) Subsequent to the opinion of the Attorney General,
however, the State Legislature, in 1985, enacted Government Code
section 1091.2 which excludes members of private industry councils
from the prohibitions of Government Code section 1090, provided
that the members comply with the provisions of Government Code
Section 87100.

3. Job Training Plan

Each SDA and its affiliated PIC are required to enter into
"an agreement or agreements" to develop a job training plan for
the SDA and to select a grant recipient to receive JTPA funds and
a grant administrator to administer the plan. (29 U.S.C. Section
1513.) Pursuant to these provisions, SETA has a formal written
agreement with PIC, which provides that SETA and PIC shall jointly
develop appropriate job training plans and that SETA shall serve
as both the local grant recipient of JTPA funds and as the local
administrator to administer the job training plan.

Each job training plan is approved independently by SETA and
PIC and submitted jointly by them to the appropriate state
officials. (29 U.S.C. Section 1514.) Each job training plan
developed under JTPA is a comprehensive document that contains a
two-year program budget and procedures for identifying and
selecting participants, performance goals, a description of
services to be provided under the plan, and "procedures for
selecting service providers which takes into account past
performance in job training or related activities." (29 U.S.C.
Section 1514.)

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 4


4.  Selecting Service Providers

     In selecting service providers within the local SDA, SETA and
PIC have historically utilized a Request for Proposal (RFP)
process whereby potential service providers (program operators)
respond to an RFP to deliver JTPA services to the community.
Program operators are, generally, community-based/nonprofit
organizations, private for-profit entities, and school districts
and other local government entities.  In addition, SETA itself
operates some programs.

     Each RFP is initially prepared by SETA staff and then
submitted to the PIC for approval or modification.  Once the PIC
has approved the RFP, it is then submitted to the SETA board for
concurrence.

     Once the RFP has been approved by the PIC and the board has
concurred in such approval, it is formally circulated to the
public for response.  Interested program operators submit formal
responses to each RFP.  After all responses are received and
reviewed, the PIC conducts a series of hearings whereby program
operators provide answers to any questions concerning their
individual proposals and, ultimately, the PIC determines which
program operators it believes should receive funds and the amount
of the funds to be allocated.  After the PIC reaches its decision,
the list of program operators is submitted to the board for
consideration and concurrence in the selection of program
operators.  Should the PIC and the board not agree, a conference
committee of the PIC and the board, comprised of equal numbers
from each group (generally two PIC members and two board members),
meets and confers until all members can agree on an allocation of
funds and selection of program operators.  The agreed upon
selection and allocation is then submitted to the entire PIC for
review and approval.  Upon such approval, it is then submitted to
the board for final concurrence.  Once the successful program
operators have been selected, the selected program operators then
enter into subgrants with SETA whereby they deliver the actual
services.

     Historically, the RFP process is repeated every year or every
two years.  Program operators who maintain programs with
demonstrated success are oftentimes able to successfully compete
for funds in subsequent years because of their past performance in
job training or related activities.  The board and the PIC must,
however, determine not only which entities receive funding in a
given funding cycle, but how the funds will be allocated among the
selected program operators.  Thus, members of the board and
members of PIC are constantly required to assess programs and vote
on how JTPA funds will be distributed to program operators.  In
situations where numerous potential program operators have
solicited funds, each member of the board and the PIC is required
to individually decide how to vote on the issue of funding for
each program operator.

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 5


     5.  On-the-Job Training

     One of the components of the JTPA program is on-the-job
training (OJT). OJT is a program pursuant to which program
operators enter into formal, written OJT contracts that provide
for the program operator to pay an employer for up to one half of
the salary of an OJT participant³ for a period not to exceed six
months. The employer agrees to provide the participant with on-
the-job training in exchange for the salary assistance. The
contract process, therefore, permits an employer to hire an
untrained employee, rather than a trained employee, thereby
allowing the participant to be trained for, and hopefully obtain,
permanent employment with the employer. Theoretically, the
program does not provide a financial advantage to the employer,
since the employer receives salary assistance in exchange for
hiring an untrained individual.

     In the funding process, the board and the PIC allocate a
portion of JTPA funds to program operators for OJT services.
After this allocation of funding is completed, the individual
program operator contracts with interested employers for actual
delivery of OJT services.

     B.  Potential Conflict of Interest

     To assist in oversight and assessment of the OJT program,
SETA and PIC have adopted a formal OJT manual. This manual
contains guidelines and procedures for administering the program
and identifies various provisions that must be contained in all
OJT contracts. Current restrictions of the SETA OJT manual, as
well as specific limitations in the OJT contract, prohibit
employers from entering into OJT contracts if any member of the
PIC is an officer, director or employee of the employer. These
restrictions exceed the requirements of the JTPA regarding
conflict of interest, which merely provide that "[n]o member of
any [private industry] council under this Act shall cast a vote on
the provision of services by that member (or any organization
which that member directly represents) or vote on any matter which
would provide direct financial benefit to that member." (29
U.S.C. Section 1551(e).) Historically, SETA's more restrictive
provisions were developed because of the prohibitions contained in
Government Code Section 1090 and general concerns of the board
regarding conflicts of interest and appearances of conflict of
interest. Due to legislative action in 1985, the Section 1090
restrictions no longer exist, provided that the individual PIC
members comply with Section 87100.

_________________________

     ³ Participants are individuals who apply for and qualify to
receive program benefits and are enrolled in a program. In the
OJT program, participants would literally become employees of the
contracting employer.

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 6


You have stated in your request for advice that recently,
members of the PIC have inquired into the legality and feasibility
of eliminating these contract restrictions and permitting
employers to enter into OJT contracts with program operators and/
or the SETA in-house program, notwithstanding the fact that a PIC
member is an officer, director or employee of the contracting
employer. Many members of the PIC have indicated that, if
permitted to do so, their employer or company would be very
interested in participating in the OJT contracting process. Since
finding willing OJT employers is always a challenge, permitting
such contracts could help the agency further its goal of finding
appropriate employment for its participants. Moreover, it would
permit PIC members to serve as examples of how an OJT program can
be effectively utilized.

You represent the governing board of SETA and seek our advice
on behalf of the members of the SETA private industry council.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest. Appointed members of the Sacramento
Employment and Training Agency (SETA) and members of private
industry councils are public officials. (Section 82048.)⁴

Economic Interests

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's
immediate family⁵ or on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.


⁴ In a memorandum dated April 22, 1986, the Commission advised
private industry councils that the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act applied to private industry council members. The
Commission also requested each private industry council to adopt a
conflict of interest code pursuant to the provisions of the Act.

⁵ An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 7

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103. (Emphasis added.)

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).) Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 8

judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
economic interests of members of SETA or of the PIC on whose
behalf you seek our advice will be materially affected by a
decision, they must not only disqualify themselves from
participating in formal vote which may affect such interests, but
they must also abstain from attempting to influence such decisions
by participating in discussions or deliberations with other
members of SETA or the PIC, or with staff, regarding the
decisions.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thornier (1975)
1 FPPC Ops 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

We have previously advised that it is reasonably foreseeable
that decisions to allocate funds to public entities with which a
member of the State Job Training Coordinating Council does
business will have a financial effect on the public agencies which
are sources of income to the public official. If this effect is
material, disqualification is required unless the "public
generally" exception applies. (Sandronsky Advice Letter, No. I-
91-435.)

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 9


     Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that funding
decisions involving a program operator who has entered into an on-
-the-job training contract with a member of the PIC or with the
member's employer will have a financial effect on the member's
economic interests.  This is so because funding decisions
concerning the program operator who has entered into a contract
with the PIC member or with the member's employer will indirectly
result in the allocation of funds to the member or the member's
employer.  If the effect of the decision will be material, as
discussed below, disqualification is required.

Materiality

     Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is
materially affected by a decision.  If the official's economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
However, when the official's economic interests will be affected
indirectly by a decision, the appropriate standards for determin-
ing materiality are those of Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6.
These regulations are discussed below.

     The initial step in assessing the materiality of the
financial effect of a governmental decision requires a
determination as to whether the involvement of the official's
economic interest is direct or indirect.  Regulation 18702.1(b)
states that a person or business entity is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person or entity,
either personally or by an agent:

          (1)  Initiates the proceeding in which the
     decision will be made by filing an application,
     claim, appeal, or similar request or;

          (2)  Is a named party in, or is the subject
     of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
     the official or the official's agency.

          (3)  A person or business entity is the
     subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
     issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
     of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
     contract with, the subject person or business
     entity.

     You have stated in your request for advice that the program
operators who receive funds from SETA include nonprofit
organizations, private for-profit entities, school districts and
other local government entities.  Decisions to allocate funds will
have a direct effect on the private and public agencies which are
the recipients of the funds because they are the subject of the
decision before SETA and the PIC.  When a source of income or

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 10

promised income to a public official is directly involved in a
decision, the appropriate standard for determining materiality is
that of regulation 18702.1 which states in pertinent part that the
effect of a decision is material if:

     Any person (including a business entity) which
     has been a source of income (including gifts) to
     the official of $250 or more in the preceding 12
     months is directly involved in a decision before
     the official's agency....

                    Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).

     Accordingly, any decision to provide funds to a program
operator which has been a source of income to a public official
participating in the decision is deemed to materially affect the
source of income. Absent application of the "public generally"
exception discussed below, disqualification is required.

Program Operators

     You have asked whether a member of SETA or of a PIC may
participate in decisions to allocate funds to a program operator
who has entered into a contract with the member to provide on-the-
job training. Under this set of facts, the program operator is a
source of promised income to the public official which will be
affected directly by the decisions to allocate funds. (Section
87103(c); Libow Advice Letter, No. I-91-461.) Accordingly, a
public official who has entered into a contract with a program
operator may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a material financial effect on the program operator.

     For example, the member must abstain from participating in
decisions and voting on awards of JTPA funds to program operators
with which the member has entered into a contract or from whom the
member has received $250 or more in income in the twelve months
preceding the decision. Moreover, the member would be required to
abstain from participating on any decision to award funds to all
program operators if such decisions would materially affect the
program operator who is or has been a source of $250 in income to
the member in the twelve months preceding the decision. The same
analysis would apply to your last inquiry in which you seek our
advice to determine whether PIC members would be required to
abstain from participating in all discussions of requests for
proposals if the member has contracted with any of the program
operators responding to the request for proposals at issue. If any
of the decisions will materially affect the program operator who
is a source of income to the official, disqualification would be
required.

     You have also asked whether, when an official's employer has
contracted with a program operator to provide on-the-job training,
the official is disqualified from participating in decisions
regarding the particular program operator. Under this set of

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 11

facts, the employer, who is a source of income to the official,
will be affected indirectly by decisions to allocate funds to the
program operator.

When a source of income to a public official will be affected
indirectly by a governmental decision, the first step in the
analysis is a determination of whether the source of income is a
for-profit business entity, a nonprofit entity including a public
entity, or an individual.

Business Entity which is a Source of Income

When a business entity which is a source of income to a
public official is indirectly affected by a governmental decision,
the appropriate standard for assessing materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.2. For all but the largest business entities,
Subdivision (g) of Regulation 18702.2 applies which states that
the effect of a decision is material for a business entity
indirectly affected by a decision if:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Accordingly, if the public official determines that granting
funds to a particular program operator which has entered into a
contract with the official's employer which is a business entity
will increase or decrease the revenues, operating expenses, or
value of the assets and liabilities of the business in the amounts
stated above, disqualification will be required.

For example, you have stated that an employer who
participates in the JTPA funding program may receive up to one
half of the salary of an employee for a period not to exceed six
months. If the salary savings for a fiscal year is $2,500 or
more, the effect of any decision to grant funds will be material.
Accordingly, an employee of a business entity which will be the
recipient of the funds would be disqualified from participating in
decisions to allocate funds to his or her employer.

Sources of Income to a Business Entity

Additionally, if a public official holds an ownership
interest of ten percent or more in a company which has contracted
with a program operator, a percentage of all income to the company

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 12

is attributed to the public official and clients of the company
are deemed sources of income to the official. (Section 82030(a);
Kolodney Advice Letter, No. I-91-462.) Accordingly, if a public
official owns a ten percent interest in a business entity, the
public official may not participate in any decision which will
have a material financial effect on a person or entity which has
provided income to the company of $2,500 or more in the twelve
months preceding a decision.⁶ (Elam Advice Letter, No. I-89-467.)
If the source of income to the company is a business entity, the
standards of Regulation 18702.2 discussed above would apply. If
the source of income is an individual, the appropriate standard
for determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.6
discussed below.

Individual who is a Source of Income

When a person who has been a source of income of $250 or more
to a public official in the twelve months preceding the decision
is indirectly involved in a decision before the official's agency,
the effect of a decision is material if:

     (a) The decision will affect the individual's
income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or

     (b) The decision will affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.

Nonprofit Entity which is a Source of Income

When a nonprofit entity, including a public governmental
entity which is a source of income to a public official will be
affected indirectly by a governmental decision, the appropriate
standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation
18702.5. We are enclosing a copy of this Regulation to aid you in
making this determination.

Public Generally

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the source of income is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.

_______________
⁶ ($2,500 x .10) = $250. If the official owns more than a ten
percent interest in the business entity, a lower level of income
from a source could result in disqualification.

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 13

(Regulation 18703.) The "public generally" comprises all persons
within the jurisdiction of the public official. (In re Owery
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 81.) Therefore, the "public generally" as
applicable to the jurisdiction of a member of SETA and its PIC
would be the County of Sacramento.

A decision will affect a source of income to the public
official in the same manner as it will affect the public generally
if it can be shown that a significant segment of the public will
be similarly affected by the decision. In the matter of In re
Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62, 67, the Commission stated that a
group that was large in numbers and heterogeneous in quality
constituted a significant segment of the public for the purposes
of the "public generally" exception. Applying these principles,
the Commission concluded that, for purposes of a rent-control
ordinance, owners of three or fewer rental units in the City of
Los Angeles constituted a significant segment of the public.

In the matter of In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, 81, the
Commission concluded that homeowners in the immediate vicinity of
the "core area" in the City of Davis constituted a significant
segment of the public. A common thread in the Commission's
analysis of this question appears to be the test first articulated
in Ferraro, supra; whether the group under consideration is large
and diverse with its only common bond being the relationship in
the grouping. Accordingly, for the "public generally" exception
to apply, the numbers affected must be sufficiently large and
sufficiently diverse.

You have stated that in some instances, school districts and
other governmental agencies are the recipients of JTPA funds. We
have previously advised that when a public entity which is a
source of income to a public official has a large and diverse
population of approximately 265,000 the "public generally"
exception applies. (Sandronsky, supra.) Any decision to allocate
funds to a city will have an effect on the entire jurisdiction of
the city and will affect a substantial segment of the public in
substantially the same manner. Accordingly, if a public entity
such as the City of Sacramento is a source of income to a SETA or
PIC member, the member may participate in decisions to allocate
funds to the city because the "public generally" exception would
apply. Therefore, public officials need not disqualify themselves
from participating in decisions which will affect a source of
income when such source of income is a public entity comprising a
significant segment of the population in the jurisdiction of the
public officials.7

Conversely, when the source of income is a business entity or
a private nonprofit corporation which will benefit from receipts
of funds, the "public generally" exception would not apply and

7 The same analysis would apply if the recipient of the funds is
a public nonprofit corporation.

Our File No. I-92-125
Page 14

disqualification would be required. This is so because the
benefit of the receipt of funds will accrue to the organization
and not to the public.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.8

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breede
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1.
Do the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Act require
members of a private industry council
(PlC)
to disqualify
themselves from participating in Job Training Partnership Act
JTPA)
funding decisions when the member or the member’s employer
has entered into an on—the-job training contract with a program
operator which is soliciting funds?
,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
 • (916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—92—125
2.
What must a PlC member do to comply with the conflict-of--
interest provisions of the Act when voting on JTPA funding
decisions when the member or the member’s employer has entered
into an on—the—job training contract with a program operator which
is soliciting funds?
Specifically:
a.
Must the PlC member abstain from voting only on
awards of JTPA funds to program operators with which the member or
the member’s employer has had a contract?
b.
Must the PlC member abstain from voting on awards of
JTPA funds to all program operators if the member or the member’s
employer has contracted with any of the program operators?
c.
Must the PlC member abstain from participating in
all discussions of requests for proposals if the member or the
member’s employer has contracted with any of the program operators
responding to the request for proposals at issue?
sections.conclusion:
1.
and 2.
Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act, public officials,
including PlC members, are disqualified
from participating in governmental decisions which will have a
material financial effect on their economic interests.
Accordingly,
members of PICs may not participate in funding
decisions which will have a material financial effect on the
member or the member’s employer.
sections.facts:
A.
Job Training Partnership Act
In 1982, the federal government enacted the Job Training
Partnership Act
(JTPA)
“to establish programs to prepare youth and
unskilled adults for entry into the labor force and to afford job
training to those economically disadvantaged individuals and other
individuals facing serious barriers to employment,
who are in
special need of such training to obtain productive employment.”
(29 U.S.C. Section 1501.)
To accomplish these goals, the
legislation provided that each state would be divided into
appropriate Service Delivery Areas
(SDAs)
“to promote effective
delivery of job training services” and to be “consistent with
areas in which related services are provided under other State or
Federal programs.”
(29 U.S.C Section 1511.)
As such,
an SDA
could be comprised of “any consortium of contiguous units of local
government with an aggregate population of 200,000 or more which
serves a substantial part of a labor market area.”
(u.)
1.
Sacramento Employment and Training Agency
Pursuant to a Joint Powers Agreement between the City of
Sacramento and the County of Sacramento, the
SDA for the city and
the county is the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency


Our File No.
1—92—125
(SETA).
SETA is operated under the control and supervision of the
SETA governing board
(the “board”)
comprised of two members of the
Sacramento County Board of Supervisors,
two members of the City
Council of the City of Sacramento and one public member.
In
addition to serving as the local SDA under the JTPA,
SETA also
administers funds under other federal and state programs such as
Head Start, the Community Service Block Grant Act, Greater Avenues
for Independence, the Refugee Targeted Assistance Program and the
Stewart B. McKinney Homeless Act.
2.
Private Industry Council
The JTPA further provided for the creation,
in each SDA,
of a
PlC, appointed by the local
SDA and comprised of “representatives
of the private sector” and “representatives of educational
agencies,
organized labor, rehabilitation agencies,
community—
based organizations,
economic development agencies and the public
employment service.”.-
(29 U.S.C. Section 1512.)
Consistent with
the requirements of the JTPA, the majority of the PlC members are
from the private sector and the chairperson of the board is
selected from among the private sector members.
In recognition of
the quasi—governmental status of PICS, it has previously been
determined by the Office of the Attorney General that members of
the PlC are public officials who must comply with the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Government Code.
(65 Ops. Att.
Gen.
41
(1982).)
Subsequent to the Opinion of the Attorney General,
however, the State Legislature,
in 1985, enacted Government Code
Section 1091.2 which excludes members of private industry councils
from the prohibitions of Government Code Section 1090, provided
that the members comply with the provisions of Government Code
Section 87100.
3.
Job Training Plan
Each SDA and its affiliated PlC are required to enter into
“an agreement or agreements” to develop a job training plan for
the
SDA and to select a grant recipient to receive JTPA funds and
a grant administrator to administer the plan.
(29 U.S.C. Section
1513.)
Pursuant to these provisions,
SETA has a formal written
agreement with PlC,
which provides that SETA and PlC shall jointly
develop appropriate job training plans and that SETA shall serve
as both the local grant recipient of JTPA funds and as the local
administrator to administer the job training plan.
Each job training plan is approved independently by SETA and
PlC and submitted jointly by them to the appropriate state
officials.
(29 U.S.C. Section 1514.)
Each job training plan
developed under JTPA is a comprehensive document that contains a
two-year program budget and procedures for identifying and
selecting participants, performance goals,
a description of
services to be provided under the plan,
and “procedures for
selecting service providers which takes into account past
performance in job training or related activities.”
(29 U.S.C.
Section 1514.)


Our File No.
1—92—125
4.
Selecting Service Providers
In selecting service providers within the local
SDA,
SETA and
PlC have historically utilized a Request for Proposal
(RFP)
process whereby potential service providers
(program operators)
respond to an RFP to deliver JTPA services to the community.
Program operators are, generally, community—based/nonprofit
organizations, private for—profit entities,
and school districts
and other local government entities.
In addition,
SETA itself
operates some programs.
Each RFP is initially prepared by SETA staff and then
submitted to the PlC for approval or modification.
Once the PlC
has approved the RFP, it is then submitted to the SETA board for
concurrence.
Once the RFP has been approved by the PlC and the board has
concurred in such approval, it is formally circulated to the
public for response.
Interested program operators submit formal
responses to each RFP.
After all responses are received and
reviewed, the PlC conducts a series of hearings whereby program
operators provide answers to any questions concerning their
individual proposals and, ultimately, the PlC determines which
program operators it believes should receive funds and the amount
of the funds to be allocated.
After the PlC reaches its decision,
the list of program operators is submitted to the board for
consideration and concurrence in the selection of program
operators.
Should the PlC and the board not agree,
a conference
committee of the PlC and the board, comprised of equal numbers
from each group (generally two PlC members and two board members),
meets and confers until its members can agree on an allocation of
funds and selection of program operators.
The agreed upon
selection and allocation is then submitted to the entire PlC for
review and approval.
Upon such approval, it is then submitted to
the board for final concurrence.
Once the successful program
operators have been selected, the selected program operators then
enter into subgrants with SETA whereby they deliver the actual
services.
Historically, the RFP process is repeated every year or every
two years.
Program operators who maintain programs with
demonstrated success are oftentimes able to successfully compete
for funds in subsequent years because of their past performance in
job training or related activities.
The board and the PlC must,
however, determine not only which entities receive funding in a
given funding cycle, but how the funds will be allocated among the
selected program operators.
Thus,
members of the board and
members of PlC are constantly required to assess programs and vote
on how JTPA funds will be distributed to program operators.
In
situations where numerous potential program operators have
solicited funds,
each member of the board and the PlC is required
to individually decide how to vote on the issue of funding for
each program operator.


Our File No.
1—92-125
5.
On-the-Job Training
One of the components of the JTPA program is on-the-job
training
(OJT).
OJT is a program pursuant to which program
operators enter into formal, written OJT contracts that provide
for the program operator to pay an employer for up to one half of
the salary of an OJT participant
3 for a period not to exceed six
months.
The employer agrees to provide the participant with on-
the—job training in exchange for the salary assistance.
The
contract process, therefore, permits an employer to hire an
untrained employee, rather than a trained employee, thereby
allowing the participant to be trained for,
and hopefully obtain,
permanent employment with the employer.
Theoretically, the
program does not provide a financial advantage to the employer,
since the employer receives salary assistance in exchange for
hiring an untrained individual.
In the funding process, the board and the PlC allocate a
portion of JTPA funds to program operators for OJT services.
After this allocation of funding is completed, the individual
program operator contracts with interested employers for actual
delivery of OJT services.
B.
Potential Conflict of Interest
To assist in oversight and assessment of the OJT program,
SETA and PlC have adopted a formal OJT manual.
This manual
contains guidelines and procedures for administering the program
and identifies various provisions that must be contained in all
OJT contracts.
Current restrictions of the SETA OJT manual,
as
well as specific limitations in the OJT contract, prohibit
employers from entering into OJT contracts if any member of the
PlC is an officer, director or employee of the employer.
These
restrictions exceed the requirements of the JTPA regarding
conflict of interest, which merely provide that “[n]o member of
any [private industry) council under this Act shall cast a vote on
the provision of services by that member
(or any organization
which that member directly represents)
or vote on any matter which
would provide direct financial benefit to that member.”
(29
U.S.C.
Section 1551(f).)
Historically,
SETA’s more restrictive
provisions were developed because of the prohibitions contained in
Government Code Section 1090 and general concerns of the board
regarding conflicts of interest and appearances of conflict of
interest.
Due to legislative action in 1985, the Section 1090
restrictions no longer exist, provided that the individual PlC
members comply with Section 87100.
Participants are individuals who apply for and qualify to
receive program benefits and are enrolled in a program.
In the
OJT program, participants would literally become employees of the
contracting employer.


Our File No.
1—92—125
You have stated in your request for advice that recently,
members of the PlC have inquired into the legality and feasibility
of eliminating these contract restrictions and permitting
employers to enter into OJT contracts with program operators and?
or the SETA in-house program, notwithstanding the fact that a PlC
member is an officer, director or employee of the contracting
employer.
Many members of the PlC have indicated that, if
permitted to do so, their employer or company would be very
interested in participating in the OJT contracting process.
Since
finding willing OJT employers is always a challenge, permitting
such contracts could help the agency further its goal of finding
appropriate employment for its participants.
Moreover, it would
permit PlC members to serve as examples of how an OJT program can
be effectively utilized.
You represent the governing board of
SETA and seek our advice
on behalf of the members of the SETA private industry council.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.
Appointed members of the Sacramento
Employment and Training Agency
(SETA)
and members of private
industry councils are public officials.
(Section 82048.)
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s
immediate family
5 or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
In a memorandam dated April 22,
1986, the Commission advised
private industry councils that the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Act applied to private industry council members.
The
Commission also requested each private industry council to adopt a
conflict of interest code pursuant to the provisions of the Act.
An official’s “immediate family” are his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No.
1-92—125
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103.
(Emphasis added.)
Making. Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision
A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter,
commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency.
(Regulation 18700(b).)
Additionally,
a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A)
Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of


Our File No.
1—92—125
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or
(B)
Preparing ar presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 18700(c).
With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official’s agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency,
an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer.
(Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
economic interests of members of SETA or of its PlC on whose
behalf you seek our advice will be materially affected by a
decision, they must not only disqualify themselves from
participating in formal votes which may affect such interests, but
they must also abstain from attempting to influence such decisions
by participating in discussions or deliberations with other
members of SETA or the PlC, or with staff, regarding the
decisions.
Foreseeability
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989—
991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
supra at 823.)
We have previously advised that it is reasonably foreseeable
that decisions to allocate funds to public entities with which a
member of the State Job Training Coordinating Council does
business will have a financial effect on the public agencies which
are sources of income to the public official.
If this effect is
material, disqualification is required unless the “public
generally” exception applies.
(Sandronsky Advice Letter,
No. I
91—435.)


Our File No.
1—92-125
Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that funding
decisions involving a program operator who has entered into an on—
-the-job training contract with a member of the PlC or with the
member’s employer will have a financial effect on the member’s
economic interests.
This is so because funding decisions
concerning the program operator who has entered into a contract
with the PlC member or with the member’s employer will indirectly
result in the allocation of funds to the member or the member’s
employer.
If the effect of the decision will be material, as
discussed below, disqualification is required.
Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official’s economic interest in a decision is
materially affected by a decision.
If the official’s economic
interest is directl’f involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
However,
when the official’s economic interests will be affected
indirectly by a decision, the appropriate standards for determin
ing materiality are those of Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6.
These regulations are discussed below.
The initial step in assessing the materiality of the
financial effect of a governmental decision requires a
determination as to whether the involvement of the official’s
economic interest is direct or indirect.
Regulation 18702.1(b)
states that a person or business entity is directly involved in a
decision before an official’s agency when that person or entity,
either personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in,
or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to,
or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
You have stated in your request for advice that the program
operators who receive funds from SETA include nonprofit
organizations, private for-profit entities,
school districts and
other local government entities.
Decisions to allocate funds will
have a direct effect on the private and public agencies which are
the recipients of the funds because they are the subject of the
decision before SETA and the PlC.
When a source of income or


Our File No.
1-92—125
promised income to a public official is directly involved in a
decision, the appropriate standard for determining materiality is
that of regulation 18702.1 which states in pertinent part that the
effect of a decision is material if:
Any person (including a business entity) which
has been a source of income (including gifts) to
the official of $250 or more in the preceding 12
months is directly involved in a decision before
the official’s agency....
Regulation 18702.1(a) (1).
Accordingly, any decision to provide funds to a program
operator which has been a source of income to a public official
participating in the decision is deemed to materially affect the
source of income.
Absent application of the “public generally”
exception discussed-below, disqualification is required.
Program Operators
You have asked whether a member of SETA or of a PlC may
participate in decisions to allocate funds to a program operator
who has entered into a contract with the member to provide on—the—
job training.
Under this set of facts, the program operator is a
source of promised income to the public official which will be
affected directly by the decisions to allocate funds.
(Section
87103(c); Libow Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—461.)
Accordingly,
a
public official who has entered into a contract with a program
operator may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a material financial effect on the program operator.
For example, the member must abstain from participating in
decisions and voting on awards of JTPA funds to program operators
with which the member has entered into a contract or from whom the
member has received $250 or more in income in the twelve months
preceding the decision.
Moreover, the member would be required to
abstain from participating on any decision to award funds to all
program operators if such decisions would materially affect the
program operator who is or has been a source of $250 in income to
the member in the twelve months preceding the decision.
The same
analysis would apply to your last inquiry in which you seek our
advice to determine whether PlC members would be required to
abstain from participating in all discussions of requests for
proposals if the member has contracted with any of the program
operators responding to the request for proposal at issue.
If any
of the decisions will materially affect the program operator who
is a source of income to the official, disqualification would be
required.
You have also asked whether,
when an official’s employer has
contracted with a program operator to provide on-the—job training,
the official is disqualified from participating in decisions
regarding the particular program operator.
Under this set of


Our File No.
1—92—125
facts, the employer,
who is a source of income to the official,
will be affected indirectly by decisions to allocate funds to the
program operator.
When a source of income to a public official will be affected
indirectly by a governmental decision, the first step in the
analysis is a determination of whether the source of income is a
for—profit business entity,
a nonprofit entity including a public
entity, or an individual.
Business Entity which is a Source of Income
When a business entity which is a source of income to a
public official is indirectly affected by a governmental decision,
the appropriate standard for assessing materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.2.
For all but the largest business entities,
Subdivision
(g)
of Regulation 18702.2 applies which states that
the effect of a decision is material for a business entity
indirectly affected by a decision if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
Accordingly, if the public official determines that granting
funds to a particular program operator which has entered into a
contract with the official’s employer which is a business entity
will increase or decrease the revenues, operating expenses,
or
value of the assets and liabilities of the business in the amounts
stated above, disqualification will be required.
For example,
you have stated that an employer who
participates in the JTPA funding program may receive up to one
half of the salary of an employee for a period not to exceed six
months.
If the salary savings for a fiscal year is $2,500 or
more, the effect of any decision to grant funds will be material.
Accordingly,
an employee of a business entity which will be the
recipient of the funds would be disqualified from participating in
decisions to allocate funds to his or her employer.
Sources of Income to a Business Entity
Additionally, if a public official holds an ownership
interest of ten percent or more in a company which has contracted
with a program operator,
a percentage of all income to the company


Our File No.
1—92—125
is attributed to the public official and clients of the company
are deemed sources of income tG the official.
(Section 82030(a);
Kolodney Advice Letter,
No. I-l-462.)
Accordingly, if a public
official owns a ten percent interest in a business entity, the
public official may not participate in any decision which will
have a material financial effect on a person or entity which has
provided income to the company of $2,500 or more in the twelve
months preceding a decision.
6
(Elam Advice Letter,
No.
1—89-467.)
If the source of income to the company is a business entity, the
standards of Regulation 18702.2 discussed above would apply.
If
the source of income is an individual, the appropriate standard
for determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.6
discussed below.
Individual who is a Source of Income
When a person ho has been a source of income of $250 or more
to a public official in the twelve months preceding the decision
is indirectly involved in a decision before the official’s agency,
the effect of a decision is material if:
(a)
The decision will affect the individual’s
income,
investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property)
by $1,000 or more; or
(b)
The decision will affect the individual’s
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.
Nonprofit Entity which is a Source of Income
When a nonprofit entity,
including a public governmental
entity which is a source of income to a public official will be
affected indirectly by a governmental decision, the appropriate
standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation
18702.5.
We are enclosing a copy of this Regulation to aid you in
making this determination.
Public Generally
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.)
If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the source of income is the
same as the effect on a significant sequent of the public.
6
($2,500 x .10)
= $250.
If the official owns more than a ten
percent interest in the business entity,
a lower level of income
from a source could result in disqualification.


Our File No.
1—92—125
(Regulation 18703.)
The “public generally” comprises all persons
within the jurisdiction of the public official.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2 FPPC Ops. 81.)
Therefore, the “public generally” as
applicable to the jurisdiction of a member of SETA and its PlC
would be the County of Sacramento.
A decision will affect a source of income to the public
official in the same manner as it will affect the public generally
if it can be shown that a significant segment of the public will
be similarly affected by the decision.
In the matter of In re
Ferraro
(1978)
4
FPPC OpS.
62,
67, the Commission stated that a
group that was large in numbers and heterogeneous in quality
constituted a significant segment of the public for the purposes
of the “public generally” exception.
Applying these principles,
the Commission concluded that, for purposes of a rent—control
ordinance,
owners of three or fewer rental units in the City of
Los Angeles constituted a significant segment of the public.
In the matter of In re Owen
(1976)
2 FPPC Ops.
77,
81, the
Commission concluded that homeowners in the immediate vicinity of
the “core area” in the City of Davis constituted a significant
segment of the public.
A common thread in the Commission’s
analysis of this question appears to be the test first articulated
in Ferraro, suDra:
whether the group under consideration is large
and diverse with its only common bond being the relationship in
the grouping.
Accordingly, for the “public generally” exception
to apply, the numbers affected must be sufficiently large and
sufficiently diverse.
You have stated that in some instances, school districts and
other governmental agencies are the recipients of JTPA funds.
We
have previously advised that when a public entity which is a
source of income to a public official has a large and diverse
population of approximately 265,000
the “public generally”
exception applies.
(Sanclronslcy, supra.)
Any decision to allocate
funds to a city will have an effect on the entire jurisdiction of
the city and will affect a substantial segment of the public in
substantially the same manner.
Accordingly, if a public entity
such as the City of Sacramento is a source of income to a SETA or
PlC member, the member may participate in decisions to allocate
funds to the city because the “public generally” exception would
apply.
Therefore, public officials need not disqualify themselves
from participating in decisions which will affect a source of
income when such source of income is a public entity comprising a
significant segment of the population in the jurisdiction of the
public officials.
7
Conversely, when the source of income is a business entity or
a private nonprofit corporation which will benefit from receipts
of funds, the “public generally” exception would not apply and
The same analysis would apply if the recipient of the funds is
a public nonprofit corporation.


Our Page
14
disqualification would be required.
This is so because the
benefit of the receipt of funds will accrue to the organization
and not to the public.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.

QUESTION: 1.
Do the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Act require
members of a private industry council
(PlC)
to disqualify
themselves from participating in Job Training Partnership Act
JTPA)
funding decisions when the member or the member’s employer
has entered into an on—the-job training contract with a program
operator which is soliciting funds?
,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
 • (916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—92—125
2.
What must a PlC member do to comply with the conflict-of--
interest provisions of the Act when voting on JTPA funding
decisions when the member or the member’s employer has entered
into an on—the—job training contract with a program operator which
is soliciting funds?
Specifically:
a.
Must the PlC member abstain from voting only on
awards of JTPA funds to program operators with which the member or
the member’s employer has had a contract?
b.
Must the PlC member abstain from voting on awards of
JTPA funds to all program operators if the member or the member’s
employer has contracted with any of the program operators?
c.
Must the PlC member abstain from participating in
all discussions of requests for proposals if the member or the
member’s employer has contracted with any of the program operators
responding to the request for proposals at issue?

CONCLUSION: 1.
and 2.
Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act, public officials,
including PlC members, are disqualified
from participating in governmental decisions which will have a
material financial effect on their economic interests.
Accordingly,
members of PICs may not participate in funding
decisions which will have a material financial effect on the
member or the member’s employer.
embedding.first_500_words: April 16, 1992 Gregory D. Thatch 1730 I Street, Suite 220 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. I-92-125 Dear Mr. Thatch: This letter is in response to your request for advice concerning the duties of members of private industry councils (PICs) under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Your request is general in nature and seeks general guidance regarding application of the provisions of the Act. Accordingly, we are providing you with informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.² Our jurisdiction is limited to application of the provisions of the Act. Therefore, we make no comments regarding other provisions of law such as Government Code Section 1090. In addition, our advice is prospective in nature and should not be interpreted as an evaluation of any past conduct. QUESTIONS 1. Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require members of a private industry council (PIC) to disqualify themselves from participating in Job Training Partnership Act (JTPA) funding decisions when the member or the member's employer has entered into an on-the-job training contract with a program operator which is soliciting funds? ________________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(C).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-92-125 Page 2 2. What must a PIC member do to comply with the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Act when voting on JTPA funding decisions when the member or the member's employer has entered into an on-the-job training contract with a program operator which is soliciting funds? Specifically: a. Must the PIC member abstain from voting only on awards of JTPA funds to program operators with which the member or the member's employer has had a contract? b. Must the PIC member abstain from voting on awards of JTPA funds to all program operators if the member or the member's employer has contracted with any of the program operators? c. Must the PIC member abstain from participating in all discussions of requests for proposals if the member or the member's employer has contracted with any of the program operators responding to the request for proposals at issue? CONCLUSIONS 1. and 2. Under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, public officials, including PIC members, are disqualified from participating in governmental decisions which will have a material financial effect on their economic interests. Accordingly, members of PICs may not participate in funding decisions which will have a material financial effect on the member or the member's employer. FACTS A. Job Training Partnership Act In 1982, the federal government enacted the Job Training Partnership Act
